The center of this 1042-millibar ridge tracked slowly on the 14th from the central Plains to the Ohio Valley.||West-northwest winds gusting to around 15-20 mph across central Indiana ushered in subzero temperatures by pre-dawn on the 14th with lows down to mainly -10 to -5 degrees across northern and central zones;  these breezes led the first, primary period of criteria wind chills for 27, mainly central to northern central Indiana, counties as well as isolated zones in southeastern central Indiana.  Wind chills fell to -20 to -32 within the 14th 100-1200 EST period, except in Montgomery County where criteria wind chills occurred through 1700 EST.||Readings on the 14th, in counties that had observed extreme wind chills, rebounded to 3 to 11 degrees.  Winds diminished by early evening, allowing temperatures to plummet under clear skies.  Temperatures falling as low as -4 to -15 degrees led a second period of criteria wind chills in 9 counties north/west of Indianapolis, within the 14th 1900 to 15th 800 EST period, although extreme wind chills ended in most zones by 400 EST.  Winds were generally under 10 mph through this secondary period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures on the morning of the 14th were as low as -3F, with sustained winds around 10 mph...resulting in marginal extreme wind chills as low as -20F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow squall produced brief heavy snow and wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph at times. The combination of the brief intense snow and strong wind gusts led to significant visibility reductions at times as the band shifted east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall produced a brief period of heavier snow and wind gusts to 35 mph at times. Significant visibility reductions to less than one quarter of a mile due to snow and blowing snow were reported with the snow squall. The Interstate 80 and Interstate 69 corridors were impacted by the snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving mid to upper level trough moved through central Indiana  on December 13th producing widespread snow across central Indiana. Snow began during the morning hours with the heaviest snowfall rates during the afternoon, before eventually tapering off into the evening. Moderate snowfall amounts of 3.0 to 5.5 inches were common, but mesoscale snow bands aided by frontogenetical forcing produced local amounts as high as 6.0 to 6.9 inches.||The Indiana State Police Indianapolis District responded to more than 300 crashes and slide-offs near the time of the event.  Any accidents responded to by local entities would have been in addition to this number.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 5.0 inches of snowfall fell, except in northeast portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow showers developed during the morning hours of November 9th and continued into the early morning hours of November 10th.  Storm total lake effect snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 14 inches, with 4 to 8 inches well inland of Lake Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy lake effect snow fell from the afternoon on November 9th into the early morning hours of November 10th. Snowfall rates were briefly as high as 1 to 2 inches per hour with the heavier lake effect snow showers.  Total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 12 inches.  A public report of 12 inches of snow was received near La Paz.  The combination of snow and gusty north winds to 30 mph at times also created some blowing and drifting snow and hazardous travel conditions. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As high pressure slowly crept northward towards the Quad State region, ridging remained over the middle Mississippi River Valley. A slow-moving MCS moved through the northeastern half of the Quad State midday to near sunset. CAPE rose to 1000-2000J/kg in Southwest Indiana with effective bulk shear around 30kts. Extremely high moisture content with precipitable water values of 2.25in resulted in torrential rainfall rates in Southwest Indiana. Each of the six counties in Southwest Indiana reported flash flooding. In Gibson County, street flooding occurred in Francisco mid-late morning, with a gage reporting 4.25in of rain 3 miles NW of Francisco. Pike County had some of the longest lasting flooding as a widespread area saw 2-3.5 during the morning hours. Spencer County received 2.5-4 of rain in and around Rockport, with flooding there lingering towards sunset. More isolated flooding issues occurred in Posey, Vanderburgh, and Warrick Counties with Evansville area flooding generally on the north and east sides of the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred in Francisco as torrential rain fell in northern Gibson County mid-morning with hourly radar estimated rainfall peaking at around 2, much of which fell after this report was made.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm produced widespread moderate to heavy wet snow November 29th into early November 30th. Total snowfall accumulations generally ranged between 6 and 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed during the late morning hours of November 29th and continued through the early morning hours of November 30th. This was a wet snow with total snow accumulations generally ranging between 7 and 11 inches. There was a report of 10.0 inches 2 miles southwest of Fort Wayne. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter of a mile at times in heavier snow. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm produced widespread moderate to heavy wet snow November 29th into early November 30th. Total snowfall accumulations generally ranged between 6 and 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed during the late morning hours of November 29th and continued through the early morning hours of November 30th. This was a wet snow with total snow accumulations generally ranging between 6 and 10 inches. There was a report of 8.0 inches in Butler. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter of a mile at times in heavier snow. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region, including a head-on collision that injured 2 people on State Road 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall dry conditions that had resulted in Severe Drought (D2) by late September 2025 over portions of 9 central Indiana counties, generally expanded and/or intensified to Extreme Drought (D3) throughout October 2025, with D2/D3 drought intensity then maintained over portions of 11 central Indiana Counties without any changes, between the October 28th and the November 25th Drought Monitor updates.||This status quo included a main sub-region that held D2+ over all of Howard County and Delaware County, as well as portions of seven other north-central to northeast central Indiana counties, from Carroll County to Randolph County, with D2 extending as far south as northern Henry County;  Two embedded areas of D3 that also included portions of northern Indiana, extended from northeast Carroll County, through most of Howard County and into extreme northern Tipton County, and also over most of Delaware County into northwestern Randolph County.  Meanwhile, a much smaller area of D2 was maintained in northwestern Vigo County and into far southwestern Vermillion County.||Precipitation throughout November for these areas did total at least 0.25 to 0.50 inches through each of the month's second, third and final weeks;  however, monthly total precipitation around only 1.50 inches for most effected areas was not enough to warrant improvement of drought intensity.  More appreciable monthly totals of 2.00-3.00 inches that neared normal amounts, over the D2 in Vermillion and Vigo Counties, prevented intensification to D3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals of only 1.10 to 1.30 inches through the first four weeks of November maintained D3 conditions over northeast and east-central Carroll County, and D2 conditions over the rest of Carroll County, excluding far southwestern corners;  overall dry conditions continued and/or expanded D2/D3 into January 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early winter storm system intensified and occluded while tracking from Kansas to the Quad Cities area from the morning to late evening of the 29th. Accumulating snow overspread northwestern and far-northern central Indiana early in the morning, and far-western zones by midday, yet a very dry 750-850 mb layer delayed the onset of snow for much of the remaining region until late afternoon. A burst of moderate to heavy snow crossed central Indiana through the early evening, with lingering light snow then changing to light rain/drizzle from south to north through the mid-evening amid the system's northward-advancing warm frontal zone.||Snowfall reached 5.0-inch criteria over eleven northwest to far-northern central Indiana counties during the late day to early evening, with observations as great as 7.0-8.0 inches along a heavier band from the Lafayette area to northwestern Howard County. The lag in snow onset through the afternoon produced a gradient in storm total snowfall between northern and central zones; mainly 1-3 inch totals across the Indianapolis Metro were led by a 3.1-inch report in northeastern Hendricks County, while 1.7 inches was the official measurement for Indianapolis, a daily record.  Despite only lighter snowfall totals, numerous traffic accidents occurred along the Interstate Route 70 corridor: in Vigo County around 1530 EST, car accidents on Interstate 70 near mile marker 15 closed all westbound lanes with no major injuries; in Putnam County, a 45-vehicle pileup occurred on Interstate 70 near Putnamville with no major injuries; in Marion County around 1940 EST, an accident on northbound Interstate Route 465 near mile marker 14 injured a state trooper inside his car; authorities reported several other traffic tie-ups in portions of north central Indiana prior to 1900 EST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 2.0 to 5.2 inches. A Travel Advisory was issued for the county due to the impacts of snow on road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow showers developed during the morning hours of November 9th and continued into the early morning hours of November 10th.  Storm total lake effect snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 14 inches, with 4 to 8 inches well inland of Lake Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy lake effect snow fell from the afternoon on November 9th into the early morning hours of November 10th. Snowfall rates were briefly as high as 1 to 2 inches per hour with the heavier lake effect snow showers. Total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 9 inches, with the highest amount in the west and northwest portions of the county. A CoCoRaHS report of 7.5 inches of snow was received 4.8 miles southwest of Elkhart. The combination of snow and gusty north winds to 30 mph at times also created some blowing and drifting snow and hazardous travel conditions. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary was positioned west to east across the Quad State during the early afternoon with convective activity increasing with daytime heating. Scattered convection primarily occurred south of the boundary mid to late afternoon. MLCAPE reached 2000-3000J/kg but effective bulk shear was very limited. Precipitable water values over 2 allowed for torrential rainfall rates. Training storm activity occurred near sunset in the Evansville/Newburgh area as isolated storms developed over the area followed by a more organized storm line. Significant flash flooding developed on the southeast side of Evanvsille and western parts of Newburgh with several streets covered by more than a foot of water. Several water rescues took place into the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwaters were slow to recede in far Southwestern Warrick County with ponding of water on roads and near homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred in Southeastern Evansville. Several spots on Lincoln Avenue east of Burkhardt Rd and on Fuquay Ave had water spilling into yards and the road, including near Plaza Park Middle School. Portions of Covert Avenue had approximately nine inches of water covering the road and over a foot of water near Greenfield Rd. Over a foot of water covered Heritage Dr, Kingswood Dr, and Lake Terrace Dr. Several inches of water were observed at the intersection of Covert Ave and I-69.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall dry conditions that had resulted in Severe Drought (D2) by late September 2025 over portions of 9 central Indiana counties, generally expanded and/or intensified to Extreme Drought (D3) throughout October 2025, with D2/D3 drought intensity then maintained over portions of 11 central Indiana Counties without any changes, between the October 28th and the November 25th Drought Monitor updates.||This status quo included a main sub-region that held D2+ over all of Howard County and Delaware County, as well as portions of seven other north-central to northeast central Indiana counties, from Carroll County to Randolph County, with D2 extending as far south as northern Henry County;  Two embedded areas of D3 that also included portions of northern Indiana, extended from northeast Carroll County, through most of Howard County and into extreme northern Tipton County, and also over most of Delaware County into northwestern Randolph County.  Meanwhile, a much smaller area of D2 was maintained in northwestern Vigo County and into far southwestern Vermillion County.||Precipitation throughout November for these areas did total at least 0.25 to 0.50 inches through each of the month's second, third and final weeks;  however, monthly total precipitation around only 1.50 inches for most effected areas was not enough to warrant improvement of drought intensity.  More appreciable monthly totals of 2.00-3.00 inches that neared normal amounts, over the D2 in Vermillion and Vigo Counties, prevented intensification to D3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals of only 0.90 to 1.30 inches through the first four weeks of November maintained D3 conditions over extreme northwestern Tipton County, and D2 conditions over most of the remaining county, excluding far southwestern areas;  overall dry conditions continued and/or expanded D2/D3 into January 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early winter storm system intensified and occluded while tracking from Kansas to the Quad Cities area from the morning to late evening of the 29th. Accumulating snow overspread northwestern and far-northern central Indiana early in the morning, and far-western zones by midday, yet a very dry 750-850 mb layer delayed the onset of snow for much of the remaining region until late afternoon. A burst of moderate to heavy snow crossed central Indiana through the early evening, with lingering light snow then changing to light rain/drizzle from south to north through the mid-evening amid the system's northward-advancing warm frontal zone.||Snowfall reached 5.0-inch criteria over eleven northwest to far-northern central Indiana counties during the late day to early evening, with observations as great as 7.0-8.0 inches along a heavier band from the Lafayette area to northwestern Howard County. The lag in snow onset through the afternoon produced a gradient in storm total snowfall between northern and central zones; mainly 1-3 inch totals across the Indianapolis Metro were led by a 3.1-inch report in northeastern Hendricks County, while 1.7 inches was the official measurement for Indianapolis, a daily record.  Despite only lighter snowfall totals, numerous traffic accidents occurred along the Interstate Route 70 corridor: in Vigo County around 1530 EST, car accidents on Interstate 70 near mile marker 15 closed all westbound lanes with no major injuries; in Putnam County, a 45-vehicle pileup occurred on Interstate 70 near Putnamville with no major injuries; in Marion County around 1940 EST, an accident on northbound Interstate Route 465 near mile marker 14 injured a state trooper inside his car; authorities reported several other traffic tie-ups in portions of north central Indiana prior to 1900 EST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 5.3 to 6.7 inches. A Travel Watch was issued for the county due to the impacts of snow on road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late during the night of the 25th into the following morning on the 26th, strong wind gusts overspread northwestern Indiana as a large winter storm departed the region. A Wind Advisory was in effect across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recorded at Gary Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow showers developed during the morning hours of November 9th and continued into the early morning hours of November 10th.  Storm total lake effect snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 14 inches, with 4 to 8 inches well inland of Lake Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy lake effect snow fell from late morning on November 9th into the early morning hours of November 10th. An estimated 5 to 10 inches of snow fell with the greatest amount across the northern part of the county.  The combination of snow and gusty north winds to 30 mph at times also created some blowing and drifting snow and hazardous travel conditions. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region the prior day with a polar air mass, causing temperatures to plummet below freezing that night. A weak mid-level low that moved through the following morning on the 10th then brought light snow showers. A quick half to one inch of snowfall was observed on cold and elevated surfaces, with variable pavement accumulation. Due to temperatures in the upper 20s, the snow caused slick spots on roadways, especially bridges and overpasses that resulted in multiple slide offs and accidents. I-64 was shut down for nearly 2 hours and backed up traffic for 5 miles while the Lloyd Expressway and highway 41 only experienced slowed traffic. In total, first responders responded to 6 crashes and 2 slide offs that caused only minor injuries and damage to personal property near Evansville, IN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As high pressure slowly crept northward towards the Quad State region, ridging remained over the middle Mississippi River Valley. A slow-moving MCS moved through the northeastern half of the Quad State midday to near sunset. CAPE rose to 1000-2000J/kg in Southwest Indiana with effective bulk shear around 30kts. Extremely high moisture content with precipitable water values of 2.25in resulted in torrential rainfall rates in Southwest Indiana. Each of the six counties in Southwest Indiana reported flash flooding. In Gibson County, street flooding occurred in Francisco mid-late morning, with a gage reporting 4.25in of rain 3 miles NW of Francisco. Pike County had some of the longest lasting flooding as a widespread area saw 2-3.5 during the morning hours. Spencer County received 2.5-4 of rain in and around Rockport, with flooding there lingering towards sunset. More isolated flooding issues occurred in Posey, Vanderburgh, and Warrick Counties with Evansville area flooding generally on the north and east sides of the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds blocking the right lane of Veterans Memorial Parkway. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous afternoon showers and thunderstorms, tracking eastward across central Indiana, had updrafts intensify from an approaching upper shortwave trough.  Effective vertical wind shear around 35-40 knots combined with freezing levels down to only 10,000 feet to promote growth of small hail.  Small hailstones falling below the freezing level congealed into isolated large hail from Sullivan County to Jackson County.  Sub-severe hail (0.75 inches) was also reported in Lawrence and Monroe Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Calm winds and lingering low-level moisture from rainfall on the 7th resulted in the formation of patchy dense fog in Spencer County early morning on the 8th.
Clearing skies and calming winds with moisture lingering from recent rainfall allowed for patchy dense fog to develop in Spencer County near sunrise.

Broad and anomolously strong 528-dm 500 mb upper trough phased and deepened while plunging into the Midwest early on the 10th.  A potent vorticity maximum, embedded at the center of the trough, tracked down Lake Michigan and into central Indiana, combining with the lake-warmed boundary layer, rather steep mid-level lapse rates of 7-8 degrees/km, and an inverted surface trough aligned near the Interstate 70 corridor, to produce numerous lake-enhanced snow showers across central Indiana.  At times heavy snowfall rates, produced 0.5 to 3.0 inch accumulations for most of the region by 700 EST, with additional snowfall amid near-record low daytime temperatures held mainly below freezing, producing storm total snowfall of 0.7-4.0 inches for most of the region by 1900 EST.||Initially wet roads became icy early on the 10th.  Multiple persons were injured in a three-vehicle crash on eastbound Interstate Route 70 near mile marker 92, in far east-central Indianapolis.  Across Marion County, the IMPD reported more than 50 crashes by 1245 EST, including seven others that caused lane closures on Interstate Routes 465 and 70.  Also reported along the I-70 corridor early on the 10th: in Hancock County during 500-815 EST 52 crashes and 15 slide-offs;  in Johnson County around 800 EST a semi trailer fell onto its side on northbound Interstate Route 65 near mile marker 98;  in Vigo County by 1000 EST 27 slide-offs/accidents;  and in Clay County around 1145 EST a semi trailer rolled over on Interstate Route 70 near mile marker 15.  The Indiana State Police Indianapolis District reported a total of 134 crashes, 18 with injuries, and 15 slide-offs across the Indianapolis Metro area through the overnight and morning of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A briefly intense snow shower reduced visibility and produced slick road surfaces, causing a 3-vehicle crash on eastbound Interstate Route 70 near mile marker 92.3.  Multiple persons were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold frontal boundary entered into the Quad State on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms formed on the south side of the front and moved eastward through the region during the afternoon and early evening hours. Afternoon storms produced severe wind gusts from straight-line winds, while a sub-severe 55mph wind gust was also observed at Deaconess Gateway Hospital. MLCAPE was around 2000J/kg, DCAPE was 1100J/kg, and effective bulk shear was 25kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A business on the corner of Oak Hill Rd and Lynch Rd had both sides of a small sign blown out by thunderstorm winds as storms tracked across Vanderburgh County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A morning frontal boundary on the northern edge of the Quad State lifted a little northward during the day on the 15th. A 40-50kt jet out of the WSW continued into the Quad State, bringing enough Gulf moisture to keep PWATs over 2 inches. MLCAPE values reached 2000-2500J/kg, while effective bulk shear remained limited. In Southwest Indiana, scattered showers and thunderstorms occurred through the day. A funnel cloud was observed from Poseyville midday, while brief flooding occurred late afternoon in Boonville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pictures were taken, from Poseyville, of a funnel cloud associated with a storm cell tracking northeastward from Wadesville to Cynthiana.

The highest totals were in Lake County where some areas received 10 to 12 inches of snowfall. 4 to 7 inches fell on northern portions of Newton and Jasper counties.

An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. Convection coverage became more isolated the 22nd-25th as the ridge strengthened over the Ohio Valley. The ridge relaxed slightly on the 26th-27th, before the worst of the heat arrived on the 28th. A cold front moving through on the 31st allowed for a much cooler and drier airmass to move across the region.||High temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to near 80) produced heat index values routinely in the 100 to 110 range from July 23rd through the 30th in Southwest Indiana, following a more off and on heat the third week of July for Southwest Indiana. Slight decreases in heat indices occurred on the 26th as more clouds were around, followed by the most oppressive heat with widespread heat indices of 110-115 on the 28th. The heat wave continued through the 30th, then a cold front moving south through the region brought relief to close out the month. Heat Advisories or Extreme Heat Warnings were in effect for portions of the Quad State for 15 consecutive days.||For the entirety of the mid-late month heatwave, Evansville, IN experienced 18 consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point, their second longest streak on record (dating back to 1948). Evansville experienced their 10th highest heat index on record on the 28th, reaching 113. Evansville observed 9 consecutive days with a heat index of at least 100 degrees from July 22nd-30th, two days shy of the record. The highest heat index in Southwest Indiana for late July was 113 at the Evansville Regional Airport ASOS on the 28th.

A slow moving cold frontal boundary entered into the Quad State on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms formed on the south side of the front and moved eastward through the region during the afternoon and early evening hours. Afternoon storms produced severe wind gusts from straight-line winds, while a sub-severe 55mph wind gust was also observed at Deaconess Gateway Hospital. MLCAPE was around 2000J/kg, DCAPE was 1100J/kg, and effective bulk shear was 25kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm tracking across Princeton produced a measured wind gust of 58mph on the south side of Princeton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong thunderstorms developed over southern Indiana in the late afternoon and moved south on the east side of a strong ridge of high pressure aloft. Southwest Indiana was in the midst of a multi-week heat wave, and the extreme heat and humidity provided fuel for the line of storms to continue south with the western end of the line passing from Oakland City to Newburgh in the early evening. A few downbursts within the line produced damaging winds near Oakland City and Otwell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down onto a camper and knocked a large tree limb down across Highway 64 just east of Oakland City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front stalled out as it moved towards the northern edge of the Quad State on the 17th. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, tracking eastward, including a broken line of storms moving through the Evansville Tri-State area mid-afternoon. An outflow boundary from a decayed MCS to the north helped initiate some of the storm development. MLCAPE was a very strong 4000J/kg but bulk shear was limited. Precipitable water values were a very high 2.25 inches. Despite an active weather day, the lack of shear kept severe weather potential limited, and the lack of training with the heavy rain kept flooding from becoming widespread. The lone report out of Southwest Indiana was of roadway ponding in Evansville from heavy rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in about a foot of standing water on the 4400 block of Bellemeade Ave in Evansville. Rainfall rates with the passing storm peaked at almost an inch of rain within thirty minutes, but the lack of additional thunderstorms/heavy rain moving over the same area prevented any flash flood development.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow fell on parts of northwestern Indiana as a compact system of low pressure moved east of Lake Michigan on the 9th and into the 10th. A majority of Lake and Porter counties received over 8 inches of accumulation. The highest totals were in Lake County where some areas received 10 to 12 inches of snowfall. 4 to 7 inches fell on northern portions of Newton and Jasper counties.

An early winter storm system intensified and occluded while tracking from Kansas to the Quad Cities area from the morning to late evening of the 29th. Accumulating snow overspread northwestern and far-northern central Indiana early in the morning, and far-western zones by midday, yet a very dry 750-850 mb layer delayed the onset of snow for much of the remaining region until late afternoon. A burst of moderate to heavy snow crossed central Indiana through the early evening, with lingering light snow then changing to light rain/drizzle from south to north through the mid-evening amid the system's northward-advancing warm frontal zone.||Snowfall reached 5.0-inch criteria over eleven northwest to far-northern central Indiana counties during the late day to early evening, with observations as great as 7.0-8.0 inches along a heavier band from the Lafayette area to northwestern Howard County. The lag in snow onset through the afternoon produced a gradient in storm total snowfall between northern and central zones; mainly 1-3 inch totals across the Indianapolis Metro were led by a 3.1-inch report in northeastern Hendricks County, while 1.7 inches was the official measurement for Indianapolis, a daily record.  Despite only lighter snowfall totals, numerous traffic accidents occurred along the Interstate Route 70 corridor: in Vigo County around 1530 EST, car accidents on Interstate 70 near mile marker 15 closed all westbound lanes with no major injuries; in Putnam County, a 45-vehicle pileup occurred on Interstate 70 near Putnamville with no major injuries; in Marion County around 1940 EST, an accident on northbound Interstate Route 465 near mile marker 14 injured a state trooper inside his car; authorities reported several other traffic tie-ups in portions of north central Indiana prior to 1900 EST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 2.6 to 4.4 inches.  Despite snowfall not reaching 5.0-inch criteria within the zone, extensive travel impacts occurred due to the holiday weekend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted a large stretch of the Midwest during the night of the 28th and through the night of the 29th. Multiple rounds of moderate to occasionally heavy snow fell on the area throughout the day. Widespread snow totals in the 6 to 9 inch range were observed across northwest Indiana.
Surface high pressure nosed southwestward across southwest Indiana. The resultant clear and calm conditions allowed dense fog to develop along portions of the Ohio River.

An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. Convection coverage became more isolated the 22nd-25th as the ridge strengthened over the Ohio Valley. The ridge relaxed slightly on the 26th-27th, before the worst of the heat arrived on the 28th. A cold front moving through on the 31st allowed for a much cooler and drier airmass to move across the region.||High temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to near 80) produced heat index values routinely in the 100 to 110 range from July 23rd through the 30th in Southwest Indiana, following a more off and on heat the third week of July for Southwest Indiana. Slight decreases in heat indices occurred on the 26th as more clouds were around, followed by the most oppressive heat with widespread heat indices of 110-115 on the 28th. The heat wave continued through the 30th, then a cold front moving south through the region brought relief to close out the month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall dry conditions that had resulted in Severe Drought (D2) by late September 2025 over portions of 9 central Indiana counties, generally expanded and/or intensified to Extreme Drought (D3) throughout October 2025, with D2/D3 drought intensity then maintained over portions of 11 central Indiana Counties without any changes, between the October 28th and the November 25th Drought Monitor updates.||This status quo included a main sub-region that held D2+ over all of Howard County and Delaware County, as well as portions of seven other north-central to northeast central Indiana counties, from Carroll County to Randolph County, with D2 extending as far south as northern Henry County;  Two embedded areas of D3 that also included portions of northern Indiana, extended from northeast Carroll County, through most of Howard County and into extreme northern Tipton County, and also over most of Delaware County into northwestern Randolph County.  Meanwhile, a much smaller area of D2 was maintained in northwestern Vigo County and into far southwestern Vermillion County.||Precipitation throughout November for these areas did total at least 0.25 to 0.50 inches through each of the month's second, third and final weeks;  however, monthly total precipitation around only 1.50 inches for most effected areas was not enough to warrant improvement of drought intensity.  More appreciable monthly totals of 2.00-3.00 inches that neared normal amounts, over the D2 in Vermillion and Vigo Counties, prevented intensification to D3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals of only 2.10 to 2.40 inches through the first four weeks of November maintained D2 conditions in extreme southwest Vermillion county, with overall dry conditions continuing and/or expanding D2 into January 2026.

As high pressure slowly crept northward towards the Quad State region, ridging remained over the middle Mississippi River Valley. A slow-moving MCS moved through the northeastern half of the Quad State midday to near sunset. CAPE rose to 1000-2000J/kg in Southwest Indiana with effective bulk shear around 30kts. Extremely high moisture content with precipitable water values of 2.25in resulted in torrential rainfall rates in Southwest Indiana. Each of the six counties in Southwest Indiana reported flash flooding. In Gibson County, street flooding occurred in Francisco mid-late morning, with a gage reporting 4.25in of rain 3 miles NW of Francisco. Pike County had some of the longest lasting flooding as a widespread area saw 2-3.5 during the morning hours. Spencer County received 2.5-4 of rain in and around Rockport, with flooding there lingering towards sunset. More isolated flooding issues occurred in Posey, Vanderburgh, and Warrick Counties with Evansville area flooding generally on the north and east sides of the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Petersburg. Radar estimated rainfall totals were approximately two inches. The report was submitted via mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm produced widespread moderate to heavy wet snow November 29th into early November 30th. Total snowfall accumulations generally ranged between 6 and 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed during the morning hours of November 29th and continued through the early morning hours of November 30th. This was a wet snow with total snow accumulations generally ranging between 7 and 11 inches. There was a report of 10.0 inches in Idaville. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter of a mile at times in heavier snow. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall dry conditions that had resulted in Severe Drought (D2) by late September 2025 over portions of 9 central Indiana counties, generally expanded and/or intensified to Extreme Drought (D3) throughout October 2025, with D2/D3 drought intensity then maintained over portions of 11 central Indiana Counties without any changes, between the October 28th and the November 25th Drought Monitor updates.||This status quo included a main sub-region that held D2+ over all of Howard County and Delaware County, as well as portions of seven other north-central to northeast central Indiana counties, from Carroll County to Randolph County, with D2 extending as far south as northern Henry County;  Two embedded areas of D3 that also included portions of northern Indiana, extended from northeast Carroll County, through most of Howard County and into extreme northern Tipton County, and also over most of Delaware County into northwestern Randolph County.  Meanwhile, a much smaller area of D2 was maintained in northwestern Vigo County and into far southwestern Vermillion County.||Precipitation throughout November for these areas did total at least 0.25 to 0.50 inches through each of the month's second, third and final weeks;  however, monthly total precipitation around only 1.50 inches for most effected areas was not enough to warrant improvement of drought intensity.  More appreciable monthly totals of 2.00-3.00 inches that neared normal amounts, over the D2 in Vermillion and Vigo Counties, prevented intensification to D3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals around only 1.20 inches through the first four weeks of November maintained D2 conditions in extreme northeast Hamilton County, with overall dry conditions continuing and/or expanding D2 into January 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early winter storm system intensified and occluded while tracking from Kansas to the Quad Cities area from the morning to late evening of the 29th. Accumulating snow overspread northwestern and far-northern central Indiana early in the morning, and far-western zones by midday, yet a very dry 750-850 mb layer delayed the onset of snow for much of the remaining region until late afternoon. A burst of moderate to heavy snow crossed central Indiana through the early evening, with lingering light snow then changing to light rain/drizzle from south to north through the mid-evening amid the system's northward-advancing warm frontal zone.||Snowfall reached 5.0-inch criteria over eleven northwest to far-northern central Indiana counties during the late day to early evening, with observations as great as 7.0-8.0 inches along a heavier band from the Lafayette area to northwestern Howard County. The lag in snow onset through the afternoon produced a gradient in storm total snowfall between northern and central zones; mainly 1-3 inch totals across the Indianapolis Metro were led by a 3.1-inch report in northeastern Hendricks County, while 1.7 inches was the official measurement for Indianapolis, a daily record.  Despite only lighter snowfall totals, numerous traffic accidents occurred along the Interstate Route 70 corridor: in Vigo County around 1530 EST, car accidents on Interstate 70 near mile marker 15 closed all westbound lanes with no major injuries; in Putnam County, a 45-vehicle pileup occurred on Interstate 70 near Putnamville with no major injuries; in Marion County around 1940 EST, an accident on northbound Interstate Route 465 near mile marker 14 injured a state trooper inside his car; authorities reported several other traffic tie-ups in portions of north central Indiana prior to 1900 EST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 3.6 to 4.5 inches.  Despite snowfall not reaching 5.0-inch criteria within the zone, extensive travel impacts occurred due to the holiday weekend.

Evansville experienced their 10th highest heat index on record on the 28th, reaching 113. An early season winter storm produced widespread moderate to heavy wet snow November 29th into early November 30th. Total snowfall accumulations generally ranged between 6 and 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed during the morning hours of November 29th and continued through the early morning hours of November 30th. This was a wet snow with total snow accumulations generally ranging between 7 and 11 inches. There was a report of 10.3 inches 3 miles southeast of Plymouth. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter of a mile at times in heavier snow. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall dry conditions that had resulted in Severe Drought (D2) by late September 2025 over portions of 9 central Indiana counties, generally expanded and/or intensified to Extreme Drought (D3) throughout October 2025, with D2/D3 drought intensity then maintained over portions of 11 central Indiana Counties without any changes, between the October 28th and the November 25th Drought Monitor updates.||This status quo included a main sub-region that held D2+ over all of Howard County and Delaware County, as well as portions of seven other north-central to northeast central Indiana counties, from Carroll County to Randolph County, with D2 extending as far south as northern Henry County;  Two embedded areas of D3 that also included portions of northern Indiana, extended from northeast Carroll County, through most of Howard County and into extreme northern Tipton County, and also over most of Delaware County into northwestern Randolph County.  Meanwhile, a much smaller area of D2 was maintained in northwestern Vigo County and into far southwestern Vermillion County.||Precipitation throughout November for these areas did total at least 0.25 to 0.50 inches through each of the month's second, third and final weeks;  however, monthly total precipitation around only 1.50 inches for most effected areas was not enough to warrant improvement of drought intensity.  More appreciable monthly totals of 2.00-3.00 inches that neared normal amounts, over the D2 in Vermillion and Vigo Counties, prevented intensification to D3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals of only 1.10 to 1.30 inches through the first four weeks of November maintained D2 conditions north and east of Frankfort, with overall dry conditions continuing and/or expanding/intensifying D2 into January 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early winter storm system intensified and occluded while tracking from Kansas to the Quad Cities area from the morning to late evening of the 29th. Accumulating snow overspread northwestern and far-northern central Indiana early in the morning, and far-western zones by midday, yet a very dry 750-850 mb layer delayed the onset of snow for much of the remaining region until late afternoon. A burst of moderate to heavy snow crossed central Indiana through the early evening, with lingering light snow then changing to light rain/drizzle from south to north through the mid-evening amid the system's northward-advancing warm frontal zone.||Snowfall reached 5.0-inch criteria over eleven northwest to far-northern central Indiana counties during the late day to early evening, with observations as great as 7.0-8.0 inches along a heavier band from the Lafayette area to northwestern Howard County. The lag in snow onset through the afternoon produced a gradient in storm total snowfall between northern and central zones; mainly 1-3 inch totals across the Indianapolis Metro were led by a 3.1-inch report in northeastern Hendricks County, while 1.7 inches was the official measurement for Indianapolis, a daily record.  Despite only lighter snowfall totals, numerous traffic accidents occurred along the Interstate Route 70 corridor: in Vigo County around 1530 EST, car accidents on Interstate 70 near mile marker 15 closed all westbound lanes with no major injuries; in Putnam County, a 45-vehicle pileup occurred on Interstate 70 near Putnamville with no major injuries; in Marion County around 1940 EST, an accident on northbound Interstate Route 465 near mile marker 14 injured a state trooper inside his car; authorities reported several other traffic tie-ups in portions of north central Indiana prior to 1900 EST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 1.2 to 3.8 inches.  A Travel Advisory was issued for the county due to the impacts of snow on road conditions;  despite snowfall not reaching 5.0-inch criteria within the zone, extensive travel impacts occurred due to the holiday weekend, including a 45-vehicle pileup occurred on Interstate 70 near Putnamville, with no major injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface low pressure system moved into Missouri, a warm front lifted into northern portions of the Quad State region. Ahead of the front, a SW to NE band of showers and thunderstorms developed, pushing eastward through mid-morning. Brief heavy rain resulted in a couple inches of water ponding on one of the Lloyd Expressway exits. Precipitable water values were 1.3in and 925-850mb moisture transport was quite strong.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple inches of water covered part of the eastbound Green River Road exit of the Lloyd Expressway. Portions of the right lanes on the expressway also had puddles form. Rainfall rates peaked at around half an inch in twenty minutes, but the heavy rainfall duration was brief.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a front pushed into northern portions of the Quad State, a SW to NE oriented line of storms organized in Southern Illinois late evening and tracked through Southwest Indiana around midnight CST. A storm developed behind that line in Posey County which went on to blow down a hardwood tree onto a barn in southwestern Evansville. MLCAPE was around 250J/kg while effective bulk shear was 30kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hardwood tree with a trunk of approximately two feet diameter was uprooted, causing damage to a barn. Small tree limbs of 3 to 5 inches in diameter were also downed. Time estimated from radar.

A northward moving warm front interacted with limited instability and shear to produce a line of showers and thunderstorms. While most remained below severe levels, one storm produced an isolated downburst with some localized damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media relayed a report of a few large trees and tree limbs snapped. A power line was down in the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought a brief period of system snow followed by lake effect snow showers across portions of northern Indiana. Total snow accumulations in the areas most impacted by lake effect snow showers ranged from 5 to 10 inches. The combination of snow and strong wind gusts produced widespread blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of system snow during the early morning of December 29th was followed by lake effect snow showers from mid morning through the evening hours. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches with most of the snow accumulation north of the Toll Road. The combination of the snow and strong wind gusts to around 45 mph led to widespread blowing and drifting snow along with near whiteout conditions at times. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the September 16th release of the drought monitor D2 (Severe drought) conditions expanded into Vermillion and Parke counties from Illinois as a result of a stretch of dry weather that began in early August. These conditions continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of only 1.55 to 2.00 inches over the month of September combined with amounts of less than an inch in August caused Severe Drought intensity to begin on September 16, 2025 in western Parke County. D2 conditions then continued into October.
